gold_final_answer: a. No such function exists. b. Yes, such a function exists.
topics_flat: Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Inverses mod n
Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Fermat / Euler / Wilson theorems
Number Theory > Residues and Primitive Roots > Primitive roots mod p / p^n
Number Theory > Residues and Primitive Roots > Multiplicative order
response_text:
Work modulo \(8\) throughout. Since only congruences mod \(8\) matter, write values in \(\mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z\).

A loggy function satisfies:

- for \(17\nmid x,y\),
\[
f(xy)\equiv f(x)+f(y)\pmod 8;
\]
- and
\[
f(x+17)\equiv f(x)\pmod 8
\]
for all \(x\).

So condition (ii) says \(f\) depends only on the residue class mod \(17\). Thus on numbers not divisible by \(17\), \(f\) induces a map
\[
g:(\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times \to \mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z
\]
such that
\[
g(ab)=g(a)+g(b).
\]
So \(g\) is a group homomorphism from the multiplicative group \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\) to the additive group \(\mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z\).

Now \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\) is cyclic of order \(16\). Hence a homomorphism to \(\mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z\) is determined by the image of a generator, and any element of \(\mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z\) may occur since every element has order dividing \(8\mid 16\).

So the question becomes: can we choose such a homomorphism with \(g(2)=1\)? and with \(g(3)=1\)?

## Key fact: \(3\) is a primitive root mod \(17\)

Indeed,
\[
3^2=9,\quad 3^4=81\equiv 13,\quad 3^8\equiv 13^2=169\equiv -1 \pmod{17},
\]
so \(3^{16}\equiv 1\) and no smaller divisor of \(16\) gives \(1\); thus \(\operatorname{ord}_{17}(3)=16\). Hence \(3\) generates \((\mathbb Z/17\mathbb Z)^\times\).

Also,
\[
3^{14}\equiv 2 \pmod{17}
\]
(since \(3^{-2}\equiv 2\), or by direct computation). Therefore in any homomorphism \(g\),
\[
g(2)=g(3^{14})=14g(3).
\]

### Part (b): can \(f(3)=1\)?

Yes. Since \(3\) is a generator of order \(16\), define a homomorphism \(g\) by
\[
g(3^k)=k \pmod 8.
\]
This is well-defined because \(3^{16}=1\) and \(16\equiv 0\pmod 8\). Then \(g(3)=1\).

Extend to all integers by periodicity mod \(17\): for \(n\not\equiv 0\pmod{17}\), let \(f(n)=g(n\bmod 17)\), and choose any value for multiples of \(17\) consistent with periodicity, e.g. \(f(17m)=0\). Then (ii) holds by construction, and (i) holds for all \(17\nmid x,y\) because \(g\) is a homomorphism.

So such a loggy function exists.

### Part (a): can \(f(2)=1\)?

Suppose such \(f\) exists, and let \(g\) be the induced homomorphism. Since \(3\) is a generator, write \(g(3)=t\in \mathbb Z/8\mathbb Z\). Then
\[
g(2)=14t\equiv 6t \pmod 8.
\]
But \(6t\) is always even mod \(8\), so it can never equal \(1\). Hence \(g(2)\neq 1\).

Therefore no such loggy function exists.

So:

- **(a)** No.
- **(b)** Yes.

Final answer: a. No; b. Yes.
